content.text: Two Complementary Governance Layers

Azure Policy and RBAC are often confused because both can produce a 403 error when they block a request. They are not alternatives — they answer different questions and must be combined for complete governance. RBAC controls which identities (users, groups, service principals, managed identities) can perform which actions. Azure Policy controls what resources created or modified by authorized identities are permitted to look like. An Owner on a subscription can still be blocked from creating a VM in an unsupported region by a Policy deny — RBAC grants the capability, Policy constrains the shape.

attrs.alt: Azure Policy vs RBAC: complementary controls diagram

content.text: The Core Distinction: WHO vs WHAT

RBAC answers the question: is this identity authorized to take this action on this resource? If not, the request is rejected with 403 Forbidden before any resource is touched. Azure Policy answers the question: does this resource configuration meet organizational standards? If not, a deny policy returns 403 RequestDisallowedByPolicy. The error code is the same but the cause is different, which is why both error types must be checked when debugging blocked deployments.

# RBAC denial: identity not authorized to create the resource at all
# Error: (AuthorizationFailed) The client 'dev@contoso.com' with object id '...'
# does not have authorization to perform action 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/write'
# over scope '/subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/rg-prod'

# Policy denial: identity IS authorized but the resource violates a policy
# Error: (RequestDisallowedByPolicy) Resource 'vm-prod-03' was disallowed by policy.
# Policy identifiers: 'allowed-vm-skus' (Deny: VM SKU 'Standard_B2ms' not in allowed list)

# Check which is causing a failure:
# 1. Verify RBAC first:
az role assignment list \
  --assignee dev@contoso.com \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod \
  --query '[].{Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}' -o table

# 2. Check what policies apply to the target scope:
az policy state list \
  --resource /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query '[].{Assignment:policyAssignmentName,Action:policyDefinitionAction}' \
  -o table
Comparison Table: Policy vs RBAC
Dimension
Azure Policy
RBAC
Controls
Resource configuration, tags, properties, regions, SKUs
Which operations (read/write/delete) an identity can perform
Applied to
Every resource in scope (identity-independent)
A specific identity (user/group/SP/MI) at a scope
Inherited by
All resources at and below the assignment scope
All resources at and below the role assignment scope
Can block an Owner?
Yes — even Owners cannot bypass a deny policy
No — Owners have all permissions; only deny assignments can restrict
Effect types
Deny, Audit, Modify, Append, DeployIfNotExists, Disabled
Allow (additive); deny assignments block specific operations
Compliance visibility
Compliance dashboard, non-compliant resource list
Access reviews (Entra ID), activity log
Auto-remediation
Yes — DINE and Modify effects can auto-fix resources
No — RBAC only controls authorization, not resource state
Tenant-wide application
Via Root Management Group assignment
Via tenant root MG role assignment (not recommended)
Third-party resources
Can evaluate custom resource types with custom policies
Applies to any ARM resource type
When Policy Blocks an Authorized User

The most common source of confusion is when a developer with Contributor role (full RBAC authorization) is blocked from creating a resource. The RBAC check passes — Contributor can create VMs — but the Policy evaluation happens after RBAC and blocks the request because the VM SKU, region, or tag configuration violates a policy. The developer needs to understand that having 'Contributor' does not mean 'can create any resource' — it means 'can create resources that also pass policy evaluation'.

# Reproduce and diagnose a policy block
# Scenario: Developer has Contributor on rg-prod but can't create vm-test-01

# Step 1: Confirm Contributor is assigned
az role assignment list \
  --assignee $(az ad signed-in-user show --query id -o tsv) \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod \
  --query '[].roleDefinitionName' -o tsv
# Output: Contributor

# Step 2: Check what policies apply at the resource group scope
az policy assignment list \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod \
  --include-inherited \
  --query '[?enforcementMode==`Default`].{Name:name,Display:displayName}' \
  -o table

# Step 3: Use what-if to see what policy evaluation would produce
az deployment group what-if \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --template-file vm-template.json \
  --parameters vmName=vm-test-01 vmSize=Standard_B2ms location=westus
# What-if output includes policy evaluation — will show the deny before deploying

# Step 4: Find the specific assignment causing the deny
az policy state list \
  --filter "resourceType eq 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query '[].{Assignment:policyAssignmentName,Action:policyDefinitionAction,Resource:resourceId}' \
  -o table

When filing an IT support ticket about a blocked deployment, always include: the exact resource ID attempted, the subscription ID, the error code (RequestDisallowedByPolicy vs AuthorizationFailed), and the policy assignment name from the error message. These four fields immediately distinguish a Policy issue from an RBAC issue and route to the right team.
What Policy Cannot Do (RBAC's Domain)

Azure Policy evaluates resource configuration — properties, tags, location, SKU — but it has no model of identity. Policy cannot restrict which users can read a storage account, cannot grant or deny specific operations, and cannot replace RBAC for authentication-based access control. These scenarios require RBAC.
Restrict who can SSH into a VM: requires NSG rules or Azure Bastion, not Policy (Policy can enforce that Bastion exists, but cannot control identity-level SSH access)
Prevent a specific user from reading secrets in Key Vault: requires Key Vault RBAC (Key Vault Reader/Secrets User roles) or Key Vault access policies
Grant a service principal write access to a storage account: requires Storage Blob Data Contributor role assignment
Prevent a developer from modifying a specific resource: requires a deny assignment at that resource's scope (RBAC), not Policy
Control who can assign roles: requires User Access Administrator role (RBAC), not Policy (Policy can audit assignments but cannot create RBAC assignments as a side effect)
What RBAC Cannot Do (Policy's Domain)

RBAC operates at the action level — it controls whether Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/write is allowed for an identity. It cannot inspect or constrain the properties of the resource being written. These scenarios require Policy.
Require all storage accounts to use HTTPS only: requires a Policy deny on allowBlobPublicAccess or supportsHttpsTrafficOnly
Block VM creation in regions outside Europe: requires an Allowed Locations policy (Policy definition with allowed-locations initiative)
Enforce that all resources have a cost-center tag: requires a Policy requiring a tag or modifying to add one
Ensure all VMs have the Log Analytics agent installed: requires a DeployIfNotExists policy (RBAC grants write access but cannot inspect installed extensions)
Audit whether storage account encryption uses customer-managed keys: requires a Policy audit — RBAC has no model of encryption configuration
Prevent creation of public IP addresses: requires a Policy deny on Microsoft.Network/publicIPAddresses
Interaction: Policy Using RBAC Internally

Some Policy effects require RBAC to function. DeployIfNotExists and Modify effects need a managed identity on the assignment — a system-assigned or user-assigned managed identity with sufficient RBAC to modify resources. Without the correct RBAC on the managed identity, the policy evaluates successfully but the remediation or modification silently fails with a 403 error in the background.

# DeployIfNotExists/Modify policy effects need RBAC for the managed identity

# 1. Create the assignment with a system-assigned managed identity
az policy assignment create \
  --name deploy-law-agent \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --policy $DINE_POLICY_DEF_ID \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus2 \
  --params '{"logAnalyticsWorkspaceId":{"value":"'$LAW_ID'"}}'

# 2. Get the managed identity's object ID
IDENTITY_OID=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name deploy-law-agent \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --query 'identity.principalId' -o tsv)

# 3. Grant the managed identity Contributor (needed for VM extension deployment)
az role assignment create \
  --assignee $IDENTITY_OID \
  --role Contributor \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID

# Without step 3, policy evaluation passes (no RBAC issue) but
# the remediation task fails with 403 on the deployment — RBAC and Policy
# are both involved in making DINE work end-to-end.
Layered Governance: Policy + RBAC Together

Production governance requires both controls layered together. RBAC defines the minimum permissions for each persona (developer, SRE, security auditor). Policy defines the constraints that apply to all of them. The combination means even an Owner cannot accidentally create a publicly-accessible storage account, place resources in a non-approved region, or skip required tags.

# Example: full governance layering for a production subscription

# RBAC layer: who can do what
# Platform Ops team: Reader at MG level (read-only across all subscriptions)
az role assignment create \
  --assignee $PLATFORM_OPS_GROUP \
  --role Reader \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp

# Product team: Owner on their subscription (full control within Policy constraints)
az role assignment create \
  --assignee $PAYMENTS_TEAM_GROUP \
  --role Owner \
  --scope /subscriptions/$PAYMENTS_SUB_ID

# Policy layer: what resources must look like (applies to ALL identities in scope)
# Even the Owner above cannot violate these:

# 1. Require tags on all resources
az policy assignment create --name require-tags \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --policy $TAG_POLICY_ID

# 2. Restrict allowed regions
az policy assignment create --name allowed-locations \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --policy /providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4f \
  --params "{"listOfAllowedLocations":{"value":["eastus2","westus2"]}}"

# 3. Block public storage accounts
az policy assignment create --name deny-public-storage \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --policy $NO_PUBLIC_STORAGE_DEF_ID

# Result: Payments team Owner can deploy any resource within approved regions,
# with required tags, without public storage — even though Owner bypasses most controls,
# Azure Policy deny assignments override RBAC Owner permissions.
Terraform: Codifying Both Layers Together

The most maintainable governance posture expresses both RBAC and Policy in the same Terraform module, making the complete access model reviewable as a single PR. The following module assigns group-based RBAC and policy assignments at the same management group scope, showing how both layers compose without overlap.

Notice that the DINE policy assignment creates a managed identity that immediately needs an RBAC role assignment. This is the clearest example of Policy and RBAC being interdependent: the policy evaluates independently, but the remediation effect requires RBAC to execute. Both must be managed together in the same module.
Auditing the Combined Governance Model

Auditing governance completeness requires checking both RBAC assignments (who has access) and Policy assignments (what is enforced). A resource can be fully compliant from a Policy perspective but still misconfigured from an RBAC perspective — for example, an external contractor added directly as a Contributor without going through the approved group process.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# governance-audit.sh — check both RBAC and Policy completeness
MG='mg-corp'
SUB='11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111'

echo '===== RBAC AUDIT ====='

echo '--- Direct User Assignments (should all be via groups) ---'
az role assignment list \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$MG \
  --include-inherited \
  --query "[?principalType=='User'].{User:principalName,Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table

echo '--- External / Guest Accounts with Role Assignments ---'
az role assignment list \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$MG \
  --include-inherited \
  --query "[?principalName contains '#EXT#'].{User:principalName,Role:roleDefinitionName}" \
  -o table

echo '--- Owner Assignments at Subscription Scope ---'
az role assignment list \
  --subscription $SUB \
  --role Owner \
  --query '[].{Principal:principalName,Type:principalType,Scope:scope}' \
  -o table

echo ''
echo '===== POLICY AUDIT ====='

echo '--- Active Assignments at Corp MG (should match Terraform state) ---'
az policy assignment list \
  --management-group $MG \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Display:displayName,Enforce:enforcementMode}' \
  -o table

echo '--- Assignments in DoNotEnforce mode (monitoring only, not blocking) ---'
az policy assignment list \
  --management-group $MG \
  --query "[?enforcementMode=='DoNotEnforce'].{Name:name,Display:displayName}" \
  -o table

echo '--- Non-Compliant Resource Count by Assignment ---'
az policy state summarize \
  --subscription $SUB \
  --query 'value[].{Assignment:policyAssignment.name,NonCompliant:results.nonCompliantResources}' \
  -o table
Common Misconceptions
Misconception
Reality
'If I'm Owner, I can do anything'
Owner has all RBAC permissions, but Azure Policy deny assignments override Owner. Owner cannot bypass a deny policy — only a Policy exemption or removing the assignment lifts the block.
'Policy is only for tagging and locations'

Policy supports 6 effects (Deny, Audit, Append, Modify, DINE, Disabled) and can evaluate any ARM property, including nested array fields, resource group tags via resourceGroup(), and ARM template functions.
'RBAC Reader prevents Policy from auditing my resources'
Policy evaluates all resources in scope regardless of who has read access. RBAC controls what the human can see; Policy evaluation runs in the Azure Policy control plane.
'Removing an RBAC assignment prevents Policy from applying'
Policy is identity-independent. Resources are evaluated against policy regardless of who deployed them or who has access to them.
'Policy and RBAC both use 'deny' — they're the same mechanism'
RBAC deny assignments block operations by identity. Policy deny blocks resource creation/update by configuration. They are enforced at different points in the ARM request pipeline.
'A policy assignment can be deleted by anyone with write access to the MG'

Deleting a policy assignment requires Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/delete permission, which is included in Owner and Contributor at the assignment's scope, but NOT in custom roles unless explicitly added.
Request Pipeline: How Policy and RBAC Interact

Understanding the ARM request pipeline shows where each control is evaluated. A request to create a resource goes through authentication, authorization (RBAC), and policy evaluation in sequence. All three gates must pass for the resource to be created. Policy evaluation happens after RBAC — so a denied RBAC request never reaches Policy, but a Policy-denied request did pass RBAC first.

# ARM request pipeline for: PUT /subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-test

# Step 1: Authentication
# ARM validates the Bearer token (Entra ID JWT). Invalid or expired token -> 401.

# Step 2: RBAC Authorization
# ARM checks if the token's identity has Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/write
# at the target scope or any parent scope.
# No permission -> 403 AuthorizationFailed

# Step 3: Azure Policy Evaluation
# If RBAC passes, ARM evaluates all applicable policy assignments.
# Policy evaluation checks the resource properties against all deny/append/modify policies.
# Deny effect triggered -> 403 RequestDisallowedByPolicy
# Append effect triggered -> resource properties modified before persisting
# Modify effect triggered -> resource properties modified (for modify-on-create policies)

# Step 4: Resource creation
# If both RBAC and Policy pass, the resource is created.
# DINE policies trigger AFTER successful creation (asynchronous).
# Audit policies record non-compliance AFTER creation (do not block).

# ── Practical consequence: ──
# A deny policy error message always includes the assignment name:
# 'Resource was disallowed by policy: require-tags (Assignment: require-tags)'
# Use this name to find the assignment and determine if an exemption is needed.
az policy assignment show \
  --name require-tags \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp \
  --query '{Display:displayName,Effect:policyRule.then.effect,Scope:scope}'
Summary

RBAC and Azure Policy are complementary governance layers that must both be applied for complete control. RBAC controls who can perform actions — it authenticates and authorizes identity-to-action relationships. Azure Policy controls what resources can look like — it constrains resource configuration, properties, tags, regions, and SKUs independent of which authorized identity is making the request. The critical insight is that an Owner can be blocked by a Policy deny: RBAC grants capability, Policy constrains shape. DINE and Modify policy effects additionally depend on RBAC — the managed identity on the assignment must have sufficient RBAC to execute the required deployment or modification. Effective governance requires defining the RBAC model (who has what permissions at which scope) and the Policy model (what all resources must conform to) as complementary and separate concerns.
